The defense announced an appeal, with Høiby maintaining he is innocent, while the prosecution framed the verdict as proof of judicial equality and victim advocates described the sentence as moderate. The case has coincided with a decline in public support for the Norwegian monarchy and will now proceed to a higher court.

item.title: DRC Ebola Outbreak Surpasses 800 Cases as Response Efforts Shift Between Rapid Diagnostics and Persistent Community Resistance
item.description: The Bundibugyo Ebola outbreak in the DRC has reached 808 confirmed cases and 192 deaths, with MSF and the DRC's National Institute of Public Health warning the epidemic remains in an active upward phase while new rapid-diagnostic tools and survivor recoveries are shifting some community attitudes toward treatment-seeking. Regional coordination efforts, vaccine development for the Bundibugyo strain, and a broader humanitarian crisis affecting children and food-insecure populations define the competing dimensions of the response.

item.title: Thousands March Against G7 in Geneva; Police Deploy Tear Gas After Clashes With Radical Bloc
item.description: An estimated 20,000 to 60,000 people marched in Geneva on June 14 against the G7 summit in nearby Évian, France, voicing grievances about global inequality, armed conflicts, and the exclusion of the Global South from decision-making. Swiss police deployed tear gas and water cannons after a contingent of radical protesters smashed windows, set a vehicle on fire, and threw projectiles at officers. The summit's composition was separately contested after South Africa's reported disinvitation under U.S. pressure, while India used the forum to press for UN Security Council reform.

item.title: Ebola Cases in Eastern DRC Surpass 700 as Community Resistance Blocks Containment in Displacement Camps
item.description: Confirmed Ebola Bundibugyo cases in the DRC have surpassed 700, with community distrust and violent resistance blocking containment efforts in displacement camps across Ituri Province. No licensed vaccine exists for the Bundibugyo strain, and while CEPI has committed $62 million to fast-track three candidates, clinical trials remain months away. The outbreak has spread into Uganda, where WHO has assessed the risk as high due to cross-border transmission.

item.title: Trump Hosts UFC Cage Fights on White House South Lawn to Mark 80th Birthday
item.description: President Trump marked his 80th birthday by hosting a UFC cage-fight event on the White House South Lawn, an event praised by administration allies as a display of American soft power and condemned by ethics groups, civil rights leaders, and polling majorities as an improper use of federal property for private commercial gain. The spectacle coincided with the announcement of a framework agreement with Iran, and reporting revealed Trump holds a financial stake in the UFC's parent company. International coverage framed the event against the backdrop of the ongoing war with Iran, rising living costs, and questions about presidential norms.

item.title: Israel Strikes Across Southern and Eastern Lebanon as US-Iran Deal Nears Signing
item.description: Israeli forces struck more than 70 Hezbollah sites across southern and eastern Lebanon and issued evacuation orders for over 20 towns, while Hezbollah launched dozens of drone and rocket attacks on Israeli positions near the border. Iran insists any deal with the United States must include an end to the war in Lebanon, a condition Israel's prime minister explicitly rejects, leaving the geographic scope of the imminent US-Iran framework agreement in dispute. UNIFIL reported the strikes violate UN Security Council Resolution 1701, and nearly 760,000 people have been displaced inside Lebanon.

item.title: Trump Sets Sunday Signing Date for Iran Deal; Tehran Says It Will Not Happen Tomorrow
item.description: President Trump announced a US-Iran deal would be signed Sunday with the Strait of Hormuz reopening immediately, while Iran's foreign ministry denied the Sunday date and described the text as a preliminary framework opening 60 days of further negotiation. Pakistan, the mediator, said finalization was expected within 24 hours, and Israel — excluded from the talks — listed four preconditions and vowed continued independent military action. Western analysts characterized the memorandum as a rough outline rather than a breakthrough, and military clashes in the strait continued even as diplomacy advanced.

item.title: US Airstrike Kills Tren de Aragua Leader Niño Guerrero in Joint Operation With Venezuela
item.description: A US airstrike killed Tren de Aragua leader Niño Guerrero in Venezuela's Bolívar state in what Washington and Caracas described as a joint counter-narcotics operation. Human rights organizations, legal scholars, and several Latin American leaders challenged the strike's lawfulness under international and US domestic law, while the US Senate advanced a bipartisan War Powers measure to restrict further unilateral military action in Venezuela. Specialist analysts noted the gang had already been fragmenting and that the killing may accelerate an internal split rather than eliminate the organization.

item.title: Federal Judge Orders Trump's Name Removed From Kennedy Center, Ruling Board Exceeded Statutory Authority
item.description: U.S. District Judge Christopher Cooper denied a stay of his order requiring the removal of President Trump's name from the Kennedy Center, ruling that the board exceeded its statutory authority under a 1964 law reserving naming power to Congress. A three-judge appeals panel, including a Trump appointee, unanimously denied an emergency motion to halt the removal, while Trump and the DOJ argued the decision was politically motivated and would harm fundraising. The appeal remains pending, and the physical removal of the name had not been completed as of the latest reports.

item.title: US Justice Department Clears Paramount's $111 Billion Acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery Without Conditions
item.description: The US Justice Department approved Paramount's $111 billion acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery without conditions, stating the deal would increase competition against dominant technology platforms. Labor unions, over 5,500 industry professionals, state attorneys general, and media-reform advocates contest the approval on antitrust, employment, and political-influence grounds, while the European Commission, the UK Competition and Markets Authority, and a coalition of US states continue independent reviews that could impose conditions or block the deal.

item.title: Pope Leo XIV Concludes Spain Visit With Condemnation of People Smugglers and Call for Legal Migration Routes
item.description: Pope Leo XIV concluded his Spain visit on June 12 with a condemnation of human traffickers and a call for safe, legal migration routes, delivered as the EU's Migration and Asylum Pact took effect. The Pope explicitly rejected the 'national priority' immigration agenda of Spain's PP and Vox parties, though both parties publicly claimed compatibility with his message. He also called for reciprocal integration, urging migrants to learn the host country's language and respect its laws while insisting that human dignity carries no passport. Pilgrims and volunteers framed the visit as a spiritual rather than political event. A technical fault grounded the papal Iberia Airbus 320, and King Felipe VI provided his personal Falcon jet for the Pope's return to Rome.

item.title: DRC Ebola Outbreak Reaches 676 Cases and 136 Deaths as Misinformation and Disputes Over US Quarantine Site in Kenya Complicate Response
item.description: The DRC Ebola outbreak caused by the Bundibugyo strain has reached 676 confirmed cases and 136 deaths across three provinces, with misinformation — including widespread denial that the disease is real — and armed conflict in Ituri complicating containment efforts. A legal and political dispute over a US-backed quarantine facility in Kenya has drawn competing claims about health sovereignty and double standards, while WHO and Africa CDC warn that only 35 percent of the $115 million requested for the response has been funded and no licensed vaccine exists for this Ebola subtype.

item.title: SpaceX Raises $75 Billion in Record IPO, Valuing Company at $1.8 Trillion and Pushing Musk Past $1 Trillion Net Worth
item.description: SpaceX raised approximately $75 billion by selling shares at $135 each, valuing the company at roughly $1.8 trillion in what multiple outlets reported as the largest IPO in history. Bullish analysts cited long-term Starlink, defense, and AI revenue potential, while skeptics warned of overvaluation, and pension officials and academics raised concerns about Musk's voting control — reported at approximately 79% in early filings and approximately 85% in later analyses — and the forced-buying risk for index-tracking funds. Chinese-language sources framed the listing as a competitive threat to China's commercial space sector, and French analysis described it as a demonstration of American financial dominance.

item.title: Trump Declares Iran War Over and Cancels Strikes; Tehran Says No Final Decision Has Been Made
item.description: President Trump announced the cancellation of planned strikes on Iran and declared a peace deal had been approved at the highest levels of Iranian leadership, but Iran's Foreign Ministry denied any final decision had been made and said US contradictions had disrupted the process. India lodged a diplomatic protest after three Indian sailors were killed in a US strike on a commercial tanker, while the Strait of Hormuz remained closed, oil prices swung, and the World Bank cut its global growth forecast to pandemic-era lows. Mediators including Qatar, the UN, and the EU called for diplomatic resolution, while a diplomat assessed a 50 percent chance the deal could collapse.

item.title: Trump Says 'I Love the Inflation' as US Consumer Prices Rise 4.2%, Fastest in Three Years
item.description: President Trump said he 'loved' the 4.2 percent inflation reading, attributing the price surge to the Iran war and predicting a collapse in costs once the conflict ends. Democratic leaders seized on the remark as evidence of indifference to household pain, while economists split on whether the May figure represents a peak or a signal that the Federal Reserve may need to hold or raise rates. Across outlets in multiple languages, the US-Israel military campaign in Iran and the resulting Strait of Hormuz disruptions were identified as the dominant driver of the energy-led price increase.

item.title: UNHCR Reports First Drop in Global Forced Displacement in a Decade as Agency Faces Budget Cuts of Nearly One-Fifth
item.description: UNHCR reported the first decline in global forced displacement in over a decade, to 117.8 million, but agency leaders and the IRC warned that most returns were unsafe and that the agency itself faces budget cuts of nearly one-fifth. Simultaneously, Human Rights Watch documented mass forced recruitment and abuse by Rwanda-backed M23 in eastern DRC, the Ebola outbreak spread to 26 health zones without an approved vaccine for the Bundibugyo strain, and Belgium rejected US demands for travel bans while accusing Washington of bearing overwhelming responsibility for Africa's deteriorating health situation. The Sahel crisis deepened the picture, with 24.3 million people in critical need and only 29 percent of required funding met.

item.title: Bill Gates Testifies Before House Oversight Committee That Jeffrey Epstein Attempted Blackmail Using Knowledge of His Extramarital Affairs
item.description: Bill Gates testified before the House Oversight Committee that Jeffrey Epstein attempted to blackmail him using knowledge of his extramarital affairs, while denying any awareness of Epstein's criminal conduct and calling the association a grave error in judgment. Democratic lawmakers split over Gates's candor, with some describing him as cooperative and others as combative and evasive, while both parties traded accusations over the investigation's partisan dynamics. The committee plans to call additional witnesses as the probe continues into the Justice Department's handling of the Epstein case.

item.title: Peru's Presidential Runoff Separated by Fewer Than 20,000 Votes as Challenged Ballots Delay Final Result
item.description: Peru's presidential runoff between Roberto Sánchez and Keiko Fujimori is separated by fewer than 20,000 votes with over 96 percent of tally sheets processed, and electoral authorities say the official result may not come until mid-July due to the review of approximately 450,000 challenged ballots. Record absenteeism exceeding 6.6 million voters has raised concerns among electoral specialists about institutional trust and democratic representation. The outcome carries direct implications for Peru's mining sector and global copper supply, with Fitch Ratings assessing divergent investment outlooks depending on which candidate prevails.

item.title: EU Proposes Entry Ban on Russian Combatants, Crypto Restrictions, and Oil Price Cap Freeze in 21st Sanctions Package
item.description: The European Commission proposed its 21st sanctions package against Russia on June 9, featuring a first-ever entry ban on Russian military personnel, a freeze on the oil price cap adjustment mechanism until January 2027, systemic restrictions on cryptocurrency infrastructure, and a ban on Russian fish imports. Human rights defenders warned that a parallel policy placing Russia on the EU's accelerated asylum-processing list could harm anti-war activists, deserters, and other vulnerable groups, while enforcement gaps including an Irish alumina loophole and Belarusian logistics circumvention schemes remain unaddressed in the current package.

item.title: Kim-Xi Summit Concludes With 'New Era' Pledge but No Mention of Denuclearisation
item.description: Xi Jinping and Kim Jong Un concluded a two-day summit in Pyongyang pledging a 'new era' of bilateral ties across politics, economy, and security, with neither leader mentioning denuclearisation or North Korea's nuclear program. Analysts across multiple regions interpret Beijing's silence on nuclear issues as tacit acceptance of Pyongyang's arsenal, while a parallel line of expert assessment frames Xi's visit as primarily aimed at countering Russia's growing influence over North Korea. The U.S. State Department maintained that Trump and Xi share the goal of denuclearisation, and South Korean President Lee Jae Myung proposed interim arms-control measures as a pragmatic alternative.

item.title: France and Germany Abandon Joint Sixth-Generation Fighter Jet After Dassault-Airbus Dispute
item.description: France and Germany formally ended the FCAS joint sixth-generation fighter jet program after Dassault Aviation and Airbus failed to resolve disputes over leadership, workshare, and intellectual property, with divergent operational requirements further complicating the partnership. Both governments stated that the networked Combat Cloud architecture will continue as a joint project, while Spain faces new procurement decisions and non-European outlets raised the prospect of increased dependence on American or other foreign fighters.

item.title: Federal Judge Strikes Down Trump's $100,000 H-1B Visa Fee as Unlawful Tax; Trump Nominates Todd Blanche as Attorney General
item.description: A federal judge in Boston struck down the Trump administration's $100,000 H-1B visa fee as an unlawful tax imposed without congressional authorization, a ruling the administration called judicial activism and pledged to appeal. Separately, Trump formally nominated his former personal lawyer Todd Blanche as attorney general, drawing Democratic opposition over DOJ independence concerns and Republican leadership support for a swift confirmation. Both developments face further proceedings — an appeals court review for the visa fee and Senate Judiciary Committee hearings for the nomination.

item.title: OpenAI Files Confidential IPO Paperwork With the SEC, Joining Anthropic in Push Toward Public Markets
item.description: OpenAI filed a confidential S-1 registration statement with the SEC on June 8, one week after Anthropic's own IPO filing, while stating it has not set a timeline or offering terms. Financial analysts are divided on whether public markets can absorb multiple trillion-dollar AI listings in close succession, with some warning of capital exhaustion and bubble-like equity concentration and others describing the filings as a natural step in financing AI infrastructure. Chinese and African analyses highlighted the filing's potential effects on global AI supply chains and technological dependency in developing economies.

item.title: Africa CDC and WHO Launch $518 Million Joint Ebola Plan as Cases Near 500 and Donor Pledges Fall Short
item.description: Africa CDC and WHO launched a joint $518 million continental response plan for the Bundibugyo Ebola outbreak, which has reached 471 confirmed cases and 84 deaths across DRC and Uganda. Donor pledges have fallen significantly short of the plan's budget, while armed conflict in eastern DRC, the absence of a licensed vaccine, and reports of unpaid frontline health workers add operational, scientific, and labor dimensions to the crisis. Governments from Tanzania to Mexico are implementing border screening and travel restrictions, while the EU has emphasized solidarity through presence and additional funding.

item.title: Xi Jinping and Kim Jong Un Hold Formal Talks in Pyongyang as Nuclear and Alliance Questions Loom
item.description: Xi Jinping and Kim Jong Un held formal talks in Pyongyang on June 8, the first China–North Korea summit in nearly seven years. Analysts across multiple regions frame the visit as Beijing's effort to reassert influence over Pyongyang amid its growing military ties with Moscow, while North Korea declared its nuclear program irreversible and the White House maintained that Washington and Beijing share a denuclearization goal. No agreements or joint statements had been announced as of the first day of meetings.

item.title: Christian Eriksen Collapses During Denmark Friendly, Reigniting Debate Over Medical Clearance for Athletes With Implanted Defibrillators
item.description: Christian Eriksen collapsed during a Denmark–Ukraine friendly, the second such incident in his career; Denmark's team doctor said his implantable cardioverter-defibrillator functioned as designed and Eriksen walked off the pitch conscious. Italian, French, Russian, and Turkish sources highlighted that several countries prohibit athletes with ICDs from professional competition, a regulatory contrast with the jurisdictions that cleared Eriksen to play. The Danish FA called on UEFA to reform its match-resumption protocols, while Eriksen remained hospitalized with the cause of the collapse still undetermined.

item.title: Israeli Airstrike Kills Three Lebanese Soldiers Including a General; Israeli Troops Shoot Dead a Seven-Month-Old Palestinian Baby in Hebron
item.description: An Israeli airstrike killed three Lebanese soldiers, including a brigadier general, in southern Lebanon — part of a broader wave of strikes Al Jazeera reported killed 12 people that day — drawing condemnation from the Lebanese government, regional states, and UNIFIL, while Israel said the vehicle moved suspiciously in a combat zone and described the strike as an error. Separately, Israeli troops shot and killed a seven-month-old Palestinian baby in Hebron after the family's car stopped as ordered, with the IDF stating soldiers perceived the vehicle accelerating toward them. Both incidents have intensified disputes over the viability of the US-brokered ceasefire, with Hezbollah rejecting the deal, analysts warning it may not hold, and Israeli opposition leaders calling it a betrayal.

item.title: Hegseth Uses D-Day Commemoration to Warn Europe of Migration 'Invasion,' Drawing Pushback from EU, UK, and French Officials
item.description: US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth used a D-Day anniversary speech at the Normandy American Cemetery to warn that Europe faces an 'invasion' of 'dangerous ideologies' through irregular migration, naming the beaches of Spain, Italy, Greece, and Bulgaria. EU Foreign Policy Chief Kaja Kallas rejected the characterization, the UK government pushed back against related remarks by Vice President JD Vance, and French Prime Minister Sébastien Lecornu used the main international ceremony — which Hegseth did not attend — to call for European strategic autonomy in defense. Historians, French local communities, and US Democratic lawmakers criticized the speech as an instrumentalization of wartime sacrifice for contemporary political purposes.

item.title: Iran Fires Ballistic Missiles at Kuwait and Bahrain After US Strikes on Iranian Radar Sites Near Strait of Hormuz
item.description: US forces struck Iranian radar sites near the Strait of Hormuz after intercepting drones, prompting the IRGC to fire ballistic missiles at US bases in Kuwait and Bahrain — attacks that killed at least one person and injured more than 60 in Kuwait. A broad coalition of Gulf, Arab, and Western states condemned Iran's strikes as violations of sovereignty, while Tehran framed them as retaliation for US aggression during a ceasefire. Negotiations remain stalled, with the US maintaining its blockade and Iran demanding the release of $24 billion in frozen assets.

item.title: Xi Jinping Arrives in Pyongyang as Kim Jong Un Showcases Naval Nuclear Ambitions
item.description: Xi Jinping arrived in Pyongyang for his first visit to North Korea since 2019, days after Kim Jong Un showcased a new destroyer as part of the country's declared nuclear war deterrence. The visit is framed by analysts across multiple regions as Beijing's bid to reassert influence over Pyongyang amid North Korea's growing ties with Moscow, while Washington and Beijing offer divergent accounts of whether they share a denuclearization goal. South Korea expressed cautious hope that China would play a constructive mediating role, and the EU characterized the Xi-Putin-Kim alignment as a challenge to the international order.

item.title: Putin Rejects Zelensky's Open Letter Proposing Direct Talks, Reaffirms Russian War Aims at St. Petersburg Forum
item.description: Vladimir Putin rejected Volodymyr Zelensky's open letter proposing direct talks, a ceasefire, and the current front line as a diplomatic baseline, calling the letter rude and stating he sees no point in meeting until expert-level agreements are reached. Zelensky and Western leaders framed the rejection as evidence that Russia does not want to end the war, while Ukrainian analysts described the letter as a strategic communication aimed at Western and global audiences. The UN Security Council is scheduled to hold an emergency meeting on June 8 at Ukraine's request.

item.title: US Strikes Iranian Radar Stations Near Strait of Hormuz; Iran Fires Seven Ballistic Missiles at US Bases in Kuwait and Bahrain
item.description: US forces struck Iranian radar installations near the Strait of Hormuz after shooting down four drones, and Iran retaliated by firing seven ballistic missiles at US bases in Kuwait and Bahrain — six of which were intercepted. Gulf states reported civilian casualties and infrastructure damage, while the World Food Program warned the Strait's disruption is driving hunger among millions in the Global South. Diplomatic positions remain far apart, with Iran demanding $24 billion in unfrozen assets and Trump insisting a deal is imminent.

item.title: Xi Jinping to Visit North Korea on June 8–9, His First Trip to Pyongyang in Nearly Seven Years
item.description: Xi Jinping will visit North Korea on June 8–9, 2026, at Kim Jong Un's invitation — his first trip to Pyongyang since 2019. Chinese official sources frame the visit as fraternal solidarity benefiting regional peace, while analysts in multiple countries interpret it as a strategic move to reassert Beijing's influence over Pyongyang amid closer North Korea–Russia ties and a quiet retreat from denuclearization language. No official agenda has been published, and South Korean, US, and North Korean government reactions remain unreported in the available record.

item.title: EU Top Court Rules Germany's Asylum Benefit Cuts Unlawful, but New Migration Pact May Permit Fresh Reductions
item.description: The European Court of Justice ruled that Germany's cuts to asylum-seeker benefits — including cash, clothing, and telecommunications funds — violate the EU's Reception Conditions Directive, establishing a minimum standard of dignified subsistence. Legal experts and advocacy groups warn, however, that the EU's new asylum and migration pact, taking effect June 12, may legally permit member states to impose new benefit reductions. The ruling arrives as EU interior ministers separately discuss ending automatic temporary protection for Ukrainian men of military age and as Germany contends with its failure to secure a UN Security Council seat.

item.title: Marjane Satrapi, Iranian-French Author of 'Persepolis,' Dies at 56
item.description: Marjane Satrapi, the Iranian-French author of Persepolis, died in Paris at 56; her family said she died of sadness following the death of her husband. French officials praised her as a towering cultural figure devoted to freedom, while Satrapi herself had publicly refused France's Legion of Honour over what she called the country's hypocritical stance toward Iranian dissidents. Persian-language diaspora sources noted that the Islamic Republic had banned her work domestically even as it won international acclaim, a fact absent from Tehran-based reporting of her death.

item.title: WHO Says Ebola Response Is 'Catching Up' as US Equipment Arrives at Kenyan Facility Despite Court Order and Protests
item.description: WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus said the Ebola response in DRC is catching up after the Bundibugyo virus circulated undetected for months, with 344 confirmed cases and 60 deaths, while Médecins Sans Frontières warned the true scale is likely far larger. Around 20 US military flights delivered equipment to a Kenyan quarantine facility despite a High Court suspension order and deadly protests, with the Kenyan and US governments defending the project and opponents demanding full disclosure of bilateral agreements. The UK launched an expert research network to support the response, and a Spanish municipality cancelled a DR Congo football friendly over health concerns.

item.title: Germany Loses UN Security Council Vote for the First Time as Austria and Portugal Secure Seats
item.description: Germany received 104 votes in the UN General Assembly election for non-permanent Security Council seats on June 3, 2026, falling short of the required two-thirds majority while Austria and Portugal won with 131 and 134 votes respectively. The German government attributed the defeat to its positions on Ukraine and Israel and accused Russia of lobbying against Berlin, while domestic opposition parties cited selective application of international law, cuts to development aid, and a late-starting campaign. Indian, East Asian, and Southeast Asian outlets framed the result as carrying implications for UN Security Council reform and as a consequence of Germany's Middle East policy.

item.title: Hungary and Ukraine Reach 11-Point Minority Rights Agreement, Clearing Path for EU Accession Negotiations
item.description: Hungary and Ukraine reached an 11-point agreement on minority rights for ethnic Hungarians in Transcarpathia, with Hungarian Prime Minister Péter Magyar announcing the deal removed Budapest's blockade of Ukraine's EU accession process. The EU's Cyprus Presidency immediately initiated preparations to open the first accession negotiation cluster, targeting June 15, though Polish and French concerns over agricultural competition may block other clusters. Magyar conditioned full support for Ukraine's accession on the completion of all 33 negotiation chapters within 10 to 15 years, followed by a Hungarian referendum.

item.title: Ebola Outbreak Reaches 344 Confirmed Cases as Kenyan Court Blocks US Quarantine Facility and Protests Turn Deadly
item.description: The DRC's Ebola outbreak has reached 344 confirmed cases and 60 deaths, while the WHO revised suspected cases downward from 906 to 116 after clearing a testing backlog. A proposed US quarantine facility in Kenya drew deadly protests, a court suspension, and competing claims from Kenyan and US officials about whom the facility would serve. No licensed vaccine exists for the Bundibugyo strain, and international organizations warn that border closures may undermine rather than support containment.

item.title: Russia Kills at Least 22 in Mass Aerial Assault on Kyiv and Dnipro; Ukraine Strikes St. Petersburg Oil Terminal During Economic Forum
item.description: Russia killed at least 22 civilians in a mass aerial assault on Kyiv and Dnipro using missiles and hundreds of drones, which Ukraine described as proof of the need for more Western air defense systems and Russia framed as retaliation for Ukrainian attacks on Russian civilians. Ukraine struck the St. Petersburg Oil Terminal during Russia's flagship economic forum, part of a drone campaign that has caused fuel rationing in occupied territories and, according to analysts, poses a serious threat to Russian oil exports. Diplomatic efforts to end the war remain stalled, with Ukraine seeking a deal before winter and the US reporting no active peace talks.

item.title: Iran Fires Missiles at Kuwait and Bahrain as OECD Warns Hormuz Blockade Could Trigger Global Recession
item.description: Iran fired missiles and drones at Kuwait and Bahrain on June 2, damaging Kuwait International Airport's passenger terminal and drawing condemnation from Gulf states, while the IRGC described the strikes as retaliation for US attacks on Qeshm Island and an Iranian tanker. The OECD warned that a prolonged Strait of Hormuz closure could reduce global GDP growth below 2 percent, while the IMF projected inflation could approach 5.8 percent under a severe scenario. US-Iran negotiations remain stalled, with Washington and Tehran offering contradictory accounts of both the military exchanges and the status of diplomatic contacts.

item.title: Anthropic Files Confidential IPO Paperwork as Alphabet Announces $80 Billion Stock Sale for AI Infrastructure
item.description: Anthropic filed a confidential IPO registration with the SEC, days after a funding round valued it at $965 billion, while Alphabet announced an $80 billion stock sale backed by a $10 billion Berkshire Hathaway investment to fund AI infrastructure. Analysts disagree on whether filing first gives Anthropic a strategic advantage or exposes it to risk, and several warn that the combined capital demands of Anthropic, OpenAI, and SpaceX could strain public markets. The filings have also raised questions about AI valuations, Anthropic's safety commitments under public-market pressure, and the impact of enterprise AI tools on sectors such as India's IT industry.

item.title: EU Agrees on Return Regulation Allowing Deportation Centers in Third Countries
item.description: The European Union reached a provisional agreement on a return regulation that creates a legal framework for deporting rejected asylum seekers to centers in third countries, with supporters calling it the missing piece of EU migration architecture and critics describing it as a systematic erosion of fundamental rights and rule-of-law principles. Italy's bilateral arrangement with Albania has served as an operational precedent, while Turkish-language and African-focused coverage frames the policy as directly affecting transit countries and as a coercive bargain with potential host nations. Implementation is expected after a 12-month adaptation period, with several EU member states already pursuing bilateral agreements with third countries.

item.title: De la Espriella Wins Colombia's First Round With 43.7%, Setting Up Polarized Runoff Against Cepeda
item.description: Abelardo de la Espriella won the first round of Colombia's presidential election with 43.7%, ahead of left-wing candidate Iván Cepeda's 40.9%, setting up a polarized runoff. President Petro's fraud allegations were challenged by electoral experts and former officials who called them unfounded, while regional right-wing leaders celebrated the result as a rejection of left-wing governance. The outcome has been framed internationally as part of a transnational far-right wave, with implications for Colombia-Venezuela relations, U.S. anti-narcotics cooperation, and the 2016 peace agreement.

item.title: Ebola Outbreak Exceeds 1,100 Suspected Cases Across DR Congo and Uganda as Suspected Cases in Europe and Brazil Test Negative
item.description: The Ebola outbreak caused by the Bundibugyo strain has exceeded 1,100 suspected cases across the DR Congo and Uganda, with Africa CDC reporting 263 confirmed cases and 43 confirmed deaths across both countries, while WHO figures show 282 confirmed cases and 42 confirmed deaths in DRC alone. Suspected cases in Brazil, Italy, and Kenya have all tested negative. WHO calls for community cooperation and an end to travel bans, while MSF warns the response has not kept pace and hundreds of samples remain untested. No licensed vaccine or therapeutic exists for the Bundibugyo strain, with clinical trials potentially months away.

item.title: US and Iran Exchange Strikes on Radar Sites and Air Bases as Ceasefire Negotiations Stall
item.description: The United States and Iran exchanged strikes on radar sites and air bases near the Strait of Hormuz despite a ceasefire nominally in effect since April, while Kuwait intercepted missiles and drones for the second time in a week. Negotiations to extend the ceasefire have stalled over disagreements on Iran's enriched uranium, frozen assets, and Strait of Hormuz access, with Trump projecting confidence in a deal that Iranian officials say remains unresolved. On a parallel front, Israel expanded military operations in Lebanon, drawing condemnation from European leaders and a French request for an emergency UN Security Council session.

item.title: French Navy Intercepts Sanctioned Russian Tanker Tagor in the Atlantic, Drawing Piracy Accusations from Moscow
item.description: The French Navy intercepted the sanctioned Russian oil tanker Tagor in the Atlantic on May 31 with allied support, marking France's fourth such operation against the shadow fleet. France and its partners framed the action as lawful sanctions enforcement under UNCLOS, while Russia called previous interceptions piracy; legal scholars cited UNCLOS Article 110 as the applicable legal basis. Reporting from Brazil and the Middle East highlighted the global scale of the shadow fleet and questions about whether fines and seizures are sufficient to deter its operations.

item.title: Ebola Cases in Eastern DR Congo Nearly Double as WHO Chief Visits Epicenter and MSF Warns Response Falls Short
item.description: The Ebola outbreak in eastern DR Congo, caused by the Bundibugyo strain for which no approved vaccine or treatment exists, has reached at least 225 confirmed cases with over 1,000 suspected cases and at least 246 deaths, and has spread to Uganda while a suspected case is under investigation in Brazil. WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus urged community-led response and opposed border closures during a visit to the epicenter, while MSF warned the response has not kept pace with what it described as one of the fastest-spreading Ebola outbreaks ever recorded. Disputes over funding cuts, a US quarantine facility in Kenya, and the absence of pharmaceutical countermeasures remain unresolved.

item.title: Israeli Forces Cross the Litani River and Seize Beaufort Castle in Deepest Lebanon Incursion in 26 Years
item.description: Israeli ground forces crossed the Litani River and seized Beaufort Castle on May 31 in the deepest incursion into Lebanon in 26 years, with the military stating the operation targets Hezbollah infrastructure. Lebanese Prime Minister Nawaf Salam condemned the advance as a scorched-earth policy of collective punishment, while Iran warned the offensive could end its ceasefire with Washington. Humanitarian agencies reported more than 3,370 killed since March 2 and nearly 700,000 displaced in a single week.

item.title: US Strikes Commercial Vessel in Gulf of Oman as Iran Reports Shooting Down American Drone and Missile Hits Kuwait Base
item.description: Three kinetic incidents — a US missile strike on a commercial vessel, Iran's reported shootdown of a US drone denied by CENTCOM, and an Iranian missile targeting of a US base in Kuwait — occurred within 48 hours of a ceasefire extension that was awaiting presidential approval. Washington framed its blockade as lawful enforcement and demanded immediate reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, while Tehran asserted sovereign authority over the waterway and accused the US of betraying diplomacy. The economic toll registered across Iran, the Gulf states, China, India, Japan, and the global insurance market, with no final agreement between the two sides confirmed.

item.title: UN Adds Israeli and Russian Forces to Sexual Violence Blacklist for First Time
item.description: The United Nations added Israeli and Russian armed and security forces to its conflict-related sexual violence blacklist for the first time, documenting verified cases against Palestinian detainees and Ukrainian prisoners of war. Israel called the listing politically motivated and severed ties with the UN Secretary-General's office, while Russia dismissed the findings as fabricated; Ukraine welcomed the decision as a step toward accountability. The report recorded nearly 10,000 cases worldwide in 2025, more than double the previous year.

item.title: Pentagon Chief Hegseth Demands Allies Spend 3.5% of GDP on Defense, Warns Against Chinese Dominance at Shangri-La Dialogue
item.description: US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth used the Shangri-La Dialogue to warn against Chinese dominance in Asia and demand that allied nations raise defense spending to 3.5 percent of GDP, prompting a formal diplomatic protest from Beijing over his Taiwan remarks. The speech exposed a tension between Hegseth's characterization of an imminent Chinese threat and President Trump's treatment of a paused Taiwan arms package as a negotiating chip. Vietnamese leader To Lam offered an alternative framework centered on international law, ASEAN centrality, and preventive diplomacy.

item.title: Federal Judge Orders Trump's Name Removed from Kennedy Center, Ruling Only Congress Can Rename the Institution
item.description: A federal judge ruled that the Kennedy Center's board of trustees acted unlawfully by adding President Trump's name to the institution and ordering a two-year closure, finding that only Congress can alter the center's designation under its 1964 founding statute. Trump attacked the ruling and announced he would transfer operational control of the center to Congress, while the Kennedy Center's leadership said it would appeal. The dispute has prompted artist boycotts, union labor charges over staff layoffs, and celebrations from the Kennedy family and the lawsuit's plaintiff, Representative Joyce Beatty.

item.title: Israel Strikes Beirut Suburbs for First Time in Weeks as Offensive Expands Beyond Southern Lebanon
item.description: Israel struck Beirut's southern suburbs for the first time in weeks — the BBC described it as the second such strike since the April ceasefire — targeting an alleged Hezbollah-allied commander, as UNIFIL recorded the highest projectile activity since the April ceasefire and hundreds of thousands of Lebanese civilians fled evacuation orders. The geographic expansion of strikes drew competing framings: Israel cited an escalating fibre-optic drone threat from Hezbollah, while Lebanese officials, humanitarian agencies, and regional actors described a humanitarian catastrophe and ceasefire violation. Security negotiations between Israeli and Lebanese military delegations were set to begin at the Pentagon on May 29 amid parallel US-Iran ceasefire talks.

item.title: US-Iran Ceasefire Extension Reportedly Agreed by Negotiators, Awaits Trump's Sign-Off
item.description: US and Iranian negotiators have reportedly agreed on a 60-day ceasefire extension covering the Strait of Hormuz, mine removal, and nuclear talks, but President Trump has not approved the memorandum and Iran's Tasnim news agency denies the text is finalized. Markets rallied on the prospect of de-escalation while military exchanges continued, and a parallel constitutional dispute over the War Powers Resolution's applicability has opened in Washington.

item.title: Russian Drone Strikes Apartment Building in NATO Member Romania, Injuring Two
item.description: A Russian drone struck an apartment building in Galați, Romania, on the night of 28–29 May, injuring two civilians in the first such incident to cause casualties on NATO territory since the start of the Ukraine war. Western leaders condemned the strike as a reckless escalation and announced new sanctions preparations, while Romania requested accelerated counter-drone capabilities and said the incident warranted NATO Article 4 consultations. Moscow did not comment directly on the Romania strike; Russian state media instead highlighted Ukrainian drone attacks on Russian territory the same night and accused Western governments of selective outrage.

item.title: Ukraine Strikes Russian Radar and Oil Targets as Zelensky Warns Trump of Critical Air Defense Shortages
item.description: Ukraine struck high-value Russian military and oil targets as part of a new 'logistical lockdown' drone campaign, while President Zelenskyy sent an urgent letter to President Trump warning of critical Patriot air defense shortages. Independent analysts described the cumulative economic damage as significant but said the direct military impact on Russia's war effort remains limited. EU foreign ministers split over whether to appoint a peace negotiator, with the bloc's foreign policy chief calling the debate a Russian 'trap,' while Germany proposed doubling NATO military aid to Ukraine.

item.title: Israel Declares Southern Lebanon a Combat Zone and Strikes Tyre as Ceasefire Framework Fractures
item.description: Israel declared all of southern Lebanon south of the Zahrani River a combat zone and launched large-scale strikes on Tyre and surrounding areas, displacing over 1.2 million people and killing dozens, including a family of six hit by a drone. Israel frames the escalation as a response to Hezbollah ceasefire violations, while Lebanese officials, European governments, UN bodies, and US senators describe the mass displacement orders and strikes as breaches of international humanitarian law and the ceasefire itself. A Lebanese military delegation is expected at Pentagon talks to present a ceasefire plan and a proposal for extending state authority across the country.

item.title: US and Iran Exchange Strikes Near Bandar Abbas as IRGC Retaliates Against American Base
item.description: The United States and Iran exchanged direct military strikes on May 28, with Washington hitting an Iranian site near Bandar Abbas and the IRGC retaliating against a US air base in Kuwait, each side accusing the other of violating the ceasefire. Oil prices surged toward $100 a barrel as the IEA warned of the worst energy security crisis in history, while the conflict spread to Kuwait, Lebanon, and the diplomatic track remained stalled over control of the Strait of Hormuz, uranium enrichment, and sanctions relief. The US Senate rejected a War Powers resolution, and analysts across multiple regions warned of lasting economic, military, and geopolitical consequences.

item.title: Germany Summons Russian Ambassador as Ukraine Strikes Russian Port and Refinery Targets
item.description: Germany, the EU, and Norway summoned Russian envoys after Moscow warned foreign diplomats to leave Kyiv ahead of announced systematic strikes, while nearly 50 countries condemned the threats at the United Nations — with the U.S. notably absent. Ukrainian drones struck Russian port, refinery, and military airfield targets overnight, extending a deep-strike campaign that has shut down the Syzran refinery and repeatedly hit Tuapse, as Ukrainian analysts dismissed Russia's threats as psychological warfare masking frontline weakness.

item.title: Iran Threatens to Strike UAE if Israel Hits Lebanon, as New US Strikes Near Hormuz Draw Accusations of Ceasefire Violation
item.description: Iran's security establishment is reportedly weighing retaliatory strikes on UAE targets in response to Israeli operations in Lebanon, adding a new escalatory dimension to the conflict as the US and Iran trade accusations over ceasefire violations near the Strait of Hormuz. South Korea linked an attack on one of its cargo ships to Iranian-developed missiles, while negotiators on both sides said a deal could take several more days. The humanitarian toll is deepening, with the WFP warning of record global hunger and Iranian civilians describing severe hardship from a prolonged internet blackout.

item.title: Israel Expands Ground Operations Deep Into Lebanon and Kills Hamas Military Chief in Gaza During Ceasefire
item.description: Israel expanded ground operations beyond its self-declared buffer zone in southern Lebanon and killed Mohammed Odeh — whom it described as Hamas military wing chief — in a Gaza City airstrike during Eid al-Adha, escalating on two fronts while ceasefires nominally remain in effect. Lebanese authorities reported at least 31 dead including children, Hezbollah claimed 32 retaliatory operations, and international bodies from the EU to China demanded Israel halt operations, while Iran warned of potential retaliation and the US simultaneously deployed advanced fighter jets to Israel and signaled progress toward a ceasefire with Tehran.
